title: GIF Freak – Client/API Architecture

children.text: Search Interface input updates the Client State Manager, which sends an Async Fetch Request to the Giphy API. The response returns through the same request layer, updates client state, and drives the Dynamic Render Engine. Read the arrows as one request-and-render cycle rather than direct UI-to-API coupling.

StockAPI ==> AppState
Firebase ==> AppState
AppState ==> ChartUI
MemeUI ==> Firebase

children.text: Stock Data API and Firebase Database feed the Central State Store, which supplies the Chart Interface. The Meme Generator writes user-created content to Firebase. The diagram separates external market data, application state, persistence, and presentation so each arrow shows which layer owns a read or write.

title: Compatibility Engine Architecture

children.text: Survey Response Data moves through Normalization, Feature Vector, Weight Matrix, and Compatibility Aggregator stages. The aggregate then feeds both the Ranking Resolver and Transparency Module. Follow the pipeline left to right: inputs become comparable features, receive explicit weights, and produce both an ordered result and its explanation.

Tech ==> Command ==> STT ==> Intent ==> Cloud ==> Resolver ==> Audio ==> Feedback ==> Tech

children.text: This system-level view shows the complete hands-free repair loop. Technician speech becomes structured intent, AWS Lambda coordinates the current repair state, and the resolver returns spoken guidance. Each physical action feeds the next command back into the same workflow.

sequenceDiagram

participant Tech as Technician
participant Mic as Voice Capture
participant STT as Speech-to-Text
participant NLP as NLP Engine
participant Lambda as AWS Lambda
participant Engine as Instruction Engine
participant Audio as Audio Output

Tech ->> Mic: Speak repair command
Mic ->> STT: Capture audio stream
STT -->> NLP: Transcribed text
NLP ->> Lambda: Structured intent
Lambda ->> Engine: Resolve current repair step
Engine -->> Audio: Next instruction
Audio -->> Tech: Spoken guidance

children.text: This sequence-level view isolates one command. Voice capture sends audio through speech-to-text and NLP, Lambda requests the current repair step from the instruction engine, and audio output returns the next instruction to the technician.

end

Editor ==> Router
Editor ==> Save

children.text: The editor keeps lesson content, code, runtime output, and saving in one browser workspace. Web projects run in a sandboxed iframe, Python exercises use an in-browser runtime, and explicit saves persist project state to S3. The split runtime paths support different lesson types without changing the student's core workflow.

User[User Identity]

subgraph Ownership[Ownership Boundary]
  Personal[Personal Projects]
  Membership[Organization Membership]
end

subgraph Governance[License Governance]
  License[Active / Expired License]
  Gate[Access Gate]
  ReadOnly[Read-Only Continuity]
end

subgraph Classroom[Institutional Usage]
  ClassroomShell[Classroom Workspace]
  Teachers[Teacher Access]
  Students[Student Access]
end

User ==> Personal
User ==> Membership
Membership ==> License ==> Gate
Gate ==>|Valid| ClassroomShell
Gate ==>|Expired| ReadOnly
ClassroomShell ==> Teachers
ClassroomShell ==> Students

children.text: The model separates user identity, personal projects, organization membership, and licensed classroom access. A license controls institutional capabilities without owning the user record. When access expires, classroom work becomes read-only while personal ownership and historical data remain intact.

Entry ==>|Student| Student
Teacher ==> Dashboard
Student ==> Dashboard
Dashboard ==> Lessons ==> Resolver
Resolver ==> Existing
Resolver ==> Clone ==> Grade

children.text: Teachers and students enter through role-specific classroom lists, then converge on the same dashboard and lesson model. Selecting a lesson sends the student project resolver down one of two paths: load existing work or clone a new template with its grading context attached.

Curriculum follows an organization-to-course-to-unit-to-lesson hierarchy. Lessons reference reusable project templates and resources, while classrooms consume the published structure without owning duplicate copies. This keeps authoring centralized and classroom delivery isolated from structural edits.
This diagram frames the library as a
state-driven interaction primitive
rather than a collection of isolated hover effects.
Base state:
provides the default interaction surface and visual contract.
Interactive transitions:
hover, focus, active, and pressed states are modeled explicitly instead of improvised per component.
Disabled behavior:
the model explicitly accounts for non-interactive states so they remain visually consistent.
The deeper value of the project is consistency: every interactive surface follows the same behavioral rules.
The theming model is designed around
fallback-based token resolution
so the primitive can be adopted in different environments without forcing one rigid theme system.
Preferred tokens:
package-level names provide the clearest public contract.
Fallback layers:
legacy, semantic, and safe-default values support incremental adoption.
Resolved output:
final values drive both the base surface and its interaction states.
Layout Style CSS sits between page requirements and the visual system. It turns repeated responsive layout work into reusable structure before color and interaction styling are applied.
Structure:
layout-style-css owns wrappers, sections, stacks, grids, and sidebars.
Paint:
ui-style-kit-css owns theme roles, palette expression, and visual surface treatment.
States:
interactive-surface-css owns hover, focus-visible, active, pressed, and disabled behavior.

sequenceDiagram

participant User
participant Client as React Client
participant API as Express API
participant DB as MongoDB

User ->> Client: Submit login form
Client ->> API: POST /api/users/login
API ->> DB: Validate credentials
DB -->> API: User found
API -->> Client: Access token + refresh cookie

Client ->> API: Request protected resource
API -->> Client: 401 when token expires

Client ->> API: POST /api/users/refresh
API -->> Client: New access token
Client ->> API: Retry queued requests
API -->> Client: Protected response

children.text: This flow shows the split between
short-lived access tokens
and a
refresh-based session path
, a core architectural concept in the template.
Access token:
used for normal authenticated API requests.
Refresh cookie:
supports session continuation without forcing constant re-login.
Retry handling:
failed requests can resume after refresh instead of immediately breaking the user flow.
The template is designed around real session behavior, not just successful login demos.
This diagram emphasizes the template’s
practical deployment model
: one server can host the API and serve the built client in production.
Frontend:
Vite handles development and build output for the React client.
Backend:
Express owns routing, auth concerns, and application hosting in production.
Operational value:
a simpler hosting story makes the starter easier to launch and reuse.

The Raspberry Pi runs a closed control loop: read DHT11 climate data, evaluate it against the selected growth-mode configuration, compare the desired and current relay states, apply only necessary hardware changes, then pause briefly before sampling again. Configuration stays separate from GPIO control so growth profiles can change without rewriting the loop.

title: Narrative Domain Architecture

children.text: Storybook is the campaign aggregate root. It contains Acts, which contain Rooms and Encounters; each Encounter references tactical context such as Opponents and Treasure. The hierarchy keeps ownership explicit while allowing a long-running narrative to grow without flattening every relationship into one record.
